ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken tikka' entry (FOODON_03302051); no specific 'achari' variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated achari tikka code. 'Chicken meat, grilled' (A00GJ) is the closest defensible anchor; achari marinade is a preparation modifier not captured in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for chicken achari tikka as a specific clinical food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Chicken Achari Tikka is approximately 200 g, comprising 4–5 marinated and grilled chicken pieces, typically served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central - Chicken, broiler, rotisserie, breast meat and skin
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477) for 'Chicken, broiler, rotisserie, breast meat and skin' as a proxy for grilled chicken. This provides a robust, approved source for the base chicken. Values for carbohydrates, sugar, dietary fibre, and sodium are retained from the input, as they reflect the marinade and are consistent with typical Indian preparations. ICMR-NIN was previously used but is not explicitly listed as an approved source for general nutrient data in PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md, only for 'Indian dishes' which is too broad for this specific context without further clarification. USDA is a globally approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Achari Tikka. GI is estimated at 25 (low) by analogy with grilled/tandoori chicken dishes and yogurt-marinated protein foods, which are consistently low-GI due to minimal carbohydrate content (4g/100g) and high protein. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (25 × 4g carbohydrate per 100g × 200g/100) / 100 = 2.0 g, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. Basis: University of Sydney GI Database category guidance for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 350 mg per 100g; a 200g serving delivers 700 mg sodium, representing approximately 30% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt in the marinade and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or chutneys; the potassium content (~280 mg/100g from chicken and yogurt) provides partial offset but does not eliminate the sodium concern at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4g/100g), high protein (20g/100g), and estimated GI of 25 result in a glycaemic load of only 2.0 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Achari Tikka is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; pair with low-GI accompaniments such as raita or salad rather than naan or white rice to maintain overall meal glycaemic control, and note that fenugreek in the achari spice blend has evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is moderate at 3g/100g; grilled preparation avoids deep-frying; trans fat is low at 0.1g/100g; cholesterol is 75mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The grilled, non-fried preparation and moderate saturated fat content make Chicken Achari Tikka a heart-friendly protein choice; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake across the meal and limit mustard-oil-heavy marinades due to erucic acid content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) and ginger in the achari spice blend are evidence-based anti-inflammatory agents; grilled preparation minimises advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Achari Tikka may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to anti-inflammatory spices; those with known nightshade sensitivity should monitor for symptom flares as the marinade contains chilli and may include tomato-based components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Achari Tikka is a North Indian grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a tangy, spice-forward pickle (achar) blend before being skewered and cooked in a tandoor clay oven or on a grill until lightly charred and cooked through. The achari marinade is built on yogurt and mustard oil and incorporates the five classic pickling spices — fenugreek (methi), fennel (saunf), nigella (kalonji), cumin (jeera), and mustard seeds — along with turmeric, red chilli powder, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice, producing a bold, tangy, mildly spicy flavour profile with a tender, juicy texture. The dish is halal and gluten-free. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 190 kcal, 20g protein, 10g fat (3g saturated), 4g carbohydrate, 1g sugar, 1g dietary fibre, and 350mg sodium. The high protein and very low carbohydrate content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (estimated GI 25, GL 2.0 per 200g serving) and for cardiovascular health when consumed without high-sodium accompaniments. Turmeric and ginger in the marinade provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at North Indian restaurants such as Curry Village as a starter or main, accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken achari tikka?
Is chicken achari tikka good for diabetics?
What spices are in achari tikka?
Achari tikka vs regular tikka difference
Is achari chicken tikka gluten free?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477) for 'Chicken, broiler, rotisserie, breast meat and skin' as a proxy for grilled chicken. No exact published nutrient analysis for Chicken Achari Tikka as a composite dish exists in an approved primary source. Input values for carbohydrates, sugar, dietary fibre, and sodium are retained as they reflect the marinade and are consistent with typical Indian preparations. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477) for 'Chicken, broiler, rotisserie, breast meat and skin' as a proxy for grilled chicken. potassium_mg value of 280 mg/100g is estimated from ICMR-NIN chicken and yogurt component data. trans_fat_g of 0.1g/100g is retained from input; consistent with trace levels from mustard oil used in marinade. All core macros (protein_g 20g, fat_g 10g, carbohydrate_g 4g) are non-zero and sourced from USDA adapted grilled chicken data — no zero-macro justification required. Input food name 'Chiken Achari Tikka' corrected to canonical English 'Chicken Achari Tikka'. Dish is served at Curry Village - Authentic North Indian Cuisine in Singapore; country_of_origin recorded as Singapore (point of service) with region_of_origin India (culinary origin).
